speaker: interviewer
text: It's tape five on March 24, 1996. And we're here today on the interview with Mr. Drexel Sprecher. I'm Bruce Einhorn. And we're in Chevy Chase, Maryland. The language is English. Was there any appeal from the decisions of the cases of either Schirach, or Fritzsche, or any of the others?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yes. An appeal was made to the Control Council for Germany by all of the defendants. [CLEARS THROAT] They each made several-- they were given, I think, two weeks, or something like this, to get them in. And the Control Council had the-- these materials translated. And they held a-- a meeting or two, and decided that they were not going to, in any way, disturb their judgment of the tribunal.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, what was the Control Council?

speaker: interviewee
text: The Control Council was made up of the four principal leaders of France, Great Britain, the United States, and the Soviet Union. The Control Council was a four-power group which, theoretically, had [CLEARS THROAT] some power after World War II to draw up legislation, or rules, which affected all four sections of the-- all occupation zones of Germany. Because of the Cold War coming on, the Control Council, more and more, had less power, because the-- you couldn't get cooperation between the Soviets and the Americans. [COUGHS] But they did enact Control Council Law Number 10, which was the basis upon which we conducted the later trials, the later 12 trials.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, did the government-- that is to say, did the four powers prosecuting these first group of cases have the authority to appeal acquittals to the Control Council? For example, Fritzsche's acquittal, could you appeal that to the Control Council?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. There was no-- no possibility of appealing an acquittal. It's almost impossible to, as you know, as a lawyer, to appeal an acquittal [CLEARS THROAT] of a defendant who's been discharged or found not guilty by any lower court.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me about some of the other defendants at the first trial.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, Goering is the first named defendant. And as the first defendant that appears, as you take a picture from the back of the courtroom toward the front, Goering was clearly the most active. He'd wave his head around and move his arms to indicate approval or disapproval of what was going on. [CLEARS THROAT] He was the most colorful of the defendants. One day, I was in court, and Justice Jackson, or his son, had asked me to be there to accompany Senator Claude Pepper of Florida, who was over visiting. And since-- since Pepper was on the Foreign Relations Committee, naturally, he had some influence. And they wanted someone to accompany him into the courtroom. So I actually took him into the courtroom on about the fourth day of the trial. And we sat at the prosecution table, back a ways from the-- the main prosecutors, who were going to present things to the tribunal, or something like that. And [CLEARS THROAT] I was looking at the dock. And I saw that Goering was writing. And he took his-- he broke his pencil. And he took it and threw it down on the dock and sat there like this. So I went up to the guard beside him and handed the guard a new pencil. And I said, Goering broke his pencil. Give him a pencil. The guard did. I went back and sat down at the prosecution table. Immediately thereafter, the court declared a recess. And Senator Pepper and I stood up between the [CLEARS THROAT] main prosecution table where he'd been sitting in the dock. And we were talking. And I had my back to the dock. And suddenly, I could notice that the Senator Pepper was bobbing his head around. He says, Goering is motioning toward you, and the guard is not objecting.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I turned around, and I walked over toward the dock. And the-- [CLEARS THROAT] Goering said to me, [SPEAKING GERMAN]. He bowed and said, I thank you, very much. I thank you, very much. And I said to him, [SPEAKING GERMAN]. You're very welcome. You're very welcome. That's the only conversation I ever had with Goering. [LAUGHS] What happened to Goering?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, Goering, of course, was sentenced to death by hanging. And he was executed after the appeal. He was executed in that manner.

speaker: interviewer
text: He was executed?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yes. I think, 10 of the 22 who were tried were actually executed. Goering committed suicide.

speaker: interviewer
text: On what charges was Goering found guilty and sentenced to death, before he committed suicide?

speaker: interviewee
text: [CLEARS THROAT] Of the conspiracy charge to wage aggressive war, crimes against-- war crimes and crimes against humanity, of various kinds.

speaker: interviewer
text: And just to refresh our understanding, he was who during the Nazi era?

speaker: interviewee
text: He was the head of the Air Force, the head of the Ministry of Aviation. He was the designated successor to Hitler, number one, appointed just after the war started, in case Hitler died. And Hess was designated as the number two defendant to succeed, the number two official to succeed Hitler or Goering, in case both of them died.

speaker: interviewer
text: And who was Hess?

speaker: interviewee
text: Hess was the-- was appointed by Hitler to be the head of the Nazi party underneath Hitler. And he was made a Reich minister without portfolio in the government. He had the right to pass notes and make memoranda on any proposals that his deputies brought to him, which were-- were proposals of Reich ministers or other people. So he kind of had a-- a-- the way of being a veto man underneath Hitler. He didn't exercise that very heavily, as far as we could determine, or as far as I remember. And he became constantly less important in the Nazi regime as time went along, because other people were put into higher positions. And they put-- the role of the party was exercised through other means, as well as the-- the role of the Nazi state.

speaker: interviewer
text: So when he flew to-- [CLEARS THROAT] to Scotland in 1941-- he went with-- I'm sure, without the permission of Hitler-- that he was trying to figure out some way to become reinstated as a great figure, by arranging some kind of a peace whereby the British, [CLEARS THROAT] in particular, would give in to the Germans. And this is well-documented as to what he wanted to do. A lot of that was not too much made a part of the record, because a lot of it was irrelevant to the charges of what he'd done before he flew to Great Britain. And of what, if anything, was he found guilty by the tribunal?

speaker: interviewee
text: He was found guilty of conspiracy and found guilty of-- of war crimes and crimes against humanity, up to that time when he flew to Scotland in 1941.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was his sentence?

speaker: interviewee
text: His sentence was a life sentence, one of three defendants who got a life sentence. And [CLEARS THROAT] he's the only one that completely served off his sentence, of those that received life sentences. The others got commutations.

speaker: interviewer
text: And is-- is Hess still alive?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, Hess died some time ago. He-- apparently his mind deteriorated, and he finally committed suicide. But this was after he was very ill and in Spandau Prison, where he was incarcerated for, I don't know how many years, but a good 30, something like that. Because he was the only defendant left after Speer and von Schirach were dismissed from prison 20 years after the trial. That'd be in be 19-- [MUMBLES] 1965.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, you had stated earlier that, of all the defendants, only Speer seemed to express any regret or responsibility. Who was Speer?

speaker: interviewee
text: I didn't quite state that.

speaker: interviewer
text: Go ahead and correct me.

speaker: interviewee
text: Several of the defendants-- and these came to be known in some circles as the Speer group-- but they included von Papen and Schacht. Schacht certainly made a lot of statements that were against the Nazis in his testimony. And of course, he had said he was willing to plea bargain. He'd lost all influence in Germany before the war. And if he'd just retired to his [CLEARS THROAT] country estate and stayed there and not appeared a time or two at public gatherings with Hitler, why, it's very likely he wouldn't even have been indicted. He felt he had to do that, he said, in order to not be persecuted further, because he was-- had fallen out of favor with the regime. And he had actually resigned as president of the Reichsbank and been replaced as Reich Minister of Economics before the war began. So the case against him was on a much weaker basis than the case against those who had been close to Hitler and who had stayed in the Nazi regime during the war.

speaker: interviewer
text: What about Speer?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, Speer had made an attempt, a rather fragile one, but had made attempt-- an attempt to assassinate Hitler in early 1945, because he was convinced that Hitler was fighting a war where nothing would happen that would benefit Germany, except that more people would be killed and more cities devastated. And I don't believe he became actively engaged with some of the opposition forces in Germany in the military, which made the attempt on Hitler's life on July 20, 1944-- I don't think he claimed that. I have no recall of that. But after he got-- after he was captured, he turned over all his-- the documents he had from the Central Planning Board and so on to his captors. And he became very willing to testify about his view concerning developments. And although he wouldn't plead guilty, he did say and wrote on the indictment, 'I think there is a certain collective responsibility for what happened,' without explaining what this all was, 'during the Nazi regime.' And he wrote that on a copy of the indictment, which the prison psychologist, Dr. Gilbert, had. And in his interrogations, he further indicated-- and his counsel didn't oppose this too much-- that he felt responsible for the slave labor program. But again, he denied knowledge that any of the slave laborers were sent to concentration camps to be exterminated after work. He denied that. And I think the tribunal, if I can just guess and what others have guessed as well, I think they felt that, although he was certainly a very powerful man from 1942 on in connection with the enslavement of people and the treatment of prisoners of war and so forth, I think the tribunal felt that this willingness to admit some collective guilt was a reason for mitigating his-- what they might have sentenced him to. They sentenced Sauckel, who had really-- almost the subordinate to Speer, in the sense that, when Speer said, I need so many hundred thousand laborers, Sauckel went out and grabbed them and brought them in from other countries and so forth. Sauckel was sentenced to death, but Speer got 20 years. The only reason I can see that being is that Speer became more contrite and more willing to disclose what had happened.

speaker: interviewer
text: What-- what had Speer been during the Nazi period?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, he was, first, Hitler's architect. And of course, that had nothing to do with the trial. But during the war, after a man by the name of Fritz Todt, T-O-D-T, who was head of the Todt Organization-- and that was an organization that took skilled laborers and unskilled, but became very much a tool of the Nazi regime to build factories, to rebuild underground places of work, and so on, after the bombings got going, and so forth. When-- when Fritz Todt died in an airplane accident, Hitler immediately put Speer in to succeed him. And he was made Reich Minister of War Production.

speaker: interviewer
text: How many defendants in all were there during the first proceedings?

speaker: interviewee
text: 24 were indicted. One committed suicide, but that was what I call my first defendant, Robert Ley, L-E-Y. Ley was head of the German labor front, which was the group which took over the free trade unions and made a kind of a inside company union, which-- in which people had no votes or anything like that. But they paid their dues. And as a result, they were allowed to travel, and were given vacation resorts, and things like that. And he was a very skilled propagandist, and had his own newspapers, and so forth.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I was making out a case against him as a part of the conspiracy for the destruction of the trade unions, and for building the German labor front, et cetera. And I had passed materials for his interrogation to both his counsel and to our interrogators before the trial began. And he wrote, in his cell, some responses to some of these things.

speaker: interviewee
text: And after-- about eight days after he was indicted, he committed suicide in his cell. Got back in a corner, sat on the toilet, tied a towel around his neck and on a fixture above him, and managed to strangle himself to death. And the guards who were passing by and looking in every now and then didn't notice this, until they noticed the-- that-- [STAMMERS] that they could just barely see his knees, or something. It looked unnatural. They opened the door, and there he was, dead.

speaker: interviewer
text: So that was my first defendant. I mean, he-- that reduced the number of defendants by one. Oh, I should say another one was dismissed just within two or three days before the trial. And that was Gustav Krupp. He was found by representatives of the tribunal who went to serve the indictment on him to be in a senile state and utterly incapable of talking. And he was dying. Who was he?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was Gustav Krupp von Bohlen und Halbach. He was the head of the huge Krupp concern, which was the greatest armament concern of all Germany. And he had been the head of a group, called the Hitler Circle of Friends, that had made vast contributions over the years to the Nazi party. There was, later on, another similar thing called, the Himmler Circle of Friends, but that's not the same thing.

speaker: interviewer
text: When were the decisions against and those not against the defendants handed down in the first proceeding? When were the judgments made?

speaker: interviewee
text: The judgments were made on October 1, 1946.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you there for that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how many--

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, the judgment was read during the first day, one whole day and the beginning of the morning on the-- well, most of the morning on the second day. And the sentences were not pronounced, until after the judges and everybody else had had lunch, including the defendants.

speaker: interviewer
text: Of the 22 defendants who ultimately faced some sort of judgement, can you give me some idea of how many were convicted of some crimes, and how many were not?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. I think, 11 of the 20 that were tried were convicted of conspiracy. Many more of them were convicted of spoliation of property of foreign countries, or slave labor, or responsibility for the concentration camps, and so forth. But I-- I couldn't tell you by numbers exactly how many. Three were found not guilty. So the total number found guilty was 19. But they were not found guilty on all counts, as I've indicated earlier. For instance, Schirach was not found guilty on count one, but was found guilty on count four. And there were several cases like that.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who were the three defendants who were completely acquitted?

speaker: interviewee
text: Franz von Papen, who had been the chancellor of Germany just before Hitler and who had helped arrange to put Hitler in power, as a part of a deal that was worked out at that time-- he was one. The defendant, Hjalmar Schacht, who had been the Reich Minister of Economics, and who'd been very active during the first years of the Nazi regime in the rearmament of Germany, but then had had a fuss with Hermann Goering and had resigned as Reich minister. And although we indicted him for his earlier activities, the tribunal found him not guilty. And the third one was Hans Fritzsche, one of my two defendants, who was found not guilty on the notion that he had not incited, intentionally, the Nazi regime to kill its opponents, and particularly the Jews.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, you mention [CLEARS THROAT] that, during your work for the first series of prosecutions before the international tribunal, you were also made a supervisor in what you have called the subsequent proceedings. Were you preparing for the subsequent proceedings while you were prosecuting the first group of proceedings?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: When did the subsequent proceedings actually begin in court? When did the prosecutions commence?

speaker: interviewee
text: The first case against the medical officers, officials, who had committed experiments on concentration camp inmates and on prisoners-- and Polish women who were not even in concentration camp had their legs cut open and all that so that they could experiment with sulfanilamide, et cetera. Go ahead. You--

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, I-- when did-- when did-- how-- when did those cases begin? When did the first presentations and the subsequent proceedings actually begin, approximately?

speaker: interviewee
text: I think that would have been December, 1946, or very early in '47, because we were very close to being ready by the end of the first trial to go into that case, as well as the Milch case, the second case. But we had-- [CLEARS THROAT] had to wait a while to-- to-- to get the translators adjusted and to get new defense counsel-- had to be appointed and have a chance to meet their defense, and so on.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where were these trials held, the subsequent cases?

speaker: interviewee
text: They were-- the 12 subsequent trials were all held in the courthouse. But-- and at one time, there was as many as four trials going on at once. The IG Farben case, in which I was chief of the IG Farben trial team, was held in the main court room, because that-- we had 24 defendants. And that was the only room which-- where you could really get that many defendants in. [COUGHS] Some of the important cases, like the military cases on the high command and the southeast, and the hostage case, were tried in a separate large room in the building, which was-- had previously not been used as a court room. Or if it-- if they had been, it'd been for very minor cases.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, we're going to go to another tape in a minute. But before we do, would you tell me which of the-- of the subsequent proceedings you were directly involved in, sir?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was involved in all the industrialist cases and to those parts of the other cases which dealt with economic matters, because I had been appointed to be head of the-- of the Economics Division. And I had used a lot of research analysts to find documents which bore on these-- these cases against Krupp, the Krupp concern, IG Farben, and Flick, and others. But those are the three that we indicted.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why don't we move to another tape now, as we go for a recess?
